description: Brewing variable interaction map. Defines how the 6 core variables affect extraction, how they interact with each other, and the priority order for adjustments per method. Grounded in SCA brewing science, Hoffmann/Rao methodology.

grind_size.what_it_controls: Surface area exposed to water. Finer = more surface = faster extraction.

direction.finer: increases extraction, slows flow (in percolation), increases body
direction.coarser: decreases extraction, speeds flow (in percolation), reduces body

grind_size.notes: The single most impactful variable in most methods. Always adjust in small increments — one notch at a time on a stepped grinder.

water_temperature.what_it_controls: Extraction energy. Hotter water dissolves compounds faster and more aggressively.

direction.hotter: increases extraction, extracts more bitter compounds at extremes
direction.cooler: decreases extraction, preserves delicate aromatics but risks under-extraction

range_guide.light_roast: 94-100C (needs more energy to extract dense beans)
range_guide.dark_roast: 85-92C (already very soluble, high temp = harsh bitterness)

water_temperature.notes: Hoffmann advocates boiling water for most filter brewing — the argument is that heat loss during pour and contact means actual slurry temp is always lower than kettle temp.

brew_time.what_it_controls: How long water is in contact with coffee. Longer = more extraction.

direction.longer: increases extraction, increases body, risk of over-extraction

brew_time.notes: In immersion methods (French press, clever dripper), you control this directly. In percolation (V60), brew time is mostly a RESULT of grind size — you don't set it, you observe it as feedback.

ratio.what_it_controls: Strength (concentration) of the final cup. Does NOT directly change extraction percentage much.

direction.more_coffee: stronger cup, slightly lower extraction (less water per gram of coffee)
direction.less_coffee: weaker cup, slightly higher extraction (more water per gram of coffee)

ratio.notes: Ratio changes STRENGTH, not extraction. A weak but over-extracted coffee is possible (too little coffee, brewed too long). Adjusting ratio is best for fixing weak/strong issues, not sour/bitter.

agitation.what_it_controls: Evenness of extraction. Moving water across grounds prevents stale concentration gradients.

direction.more: increases extraction speed and evenness, risk of over-agitation causing fines migration and channeling
direction.less: slower, less even extraction, can lead to some grounds under-extracted

forms: pour pattern (concentric circles vs center pour)
agitation.notes: Most under-appreciated variable. Consistency matters more than amount — do the same thing every time so you can isolate other variables.

water_quality.what_it_controls: How efficiently water extracts coffee solubles. Mineral content acts as a catalyst.

direction.harder_water: extracts more, amplifies bitterness, can taste chalky or flat
direction.softer_water: extracts less, brighter acidity, can taste sour and empty if too soft

water_quality.notes: If someone is doing everything right and coffee still tastes flat or harsh, water is the likely culprit. Use good quality filtered or bottled water — avoid distilled or heavily softened water.

interactions.rule: Adjust only ONE at a time. Both increase extraction in the same direction. Changing both simultaneously will overshoot.
interactions.example: If coffee is sour, grind finer OR increase temp — not both. Taste, then decide if you need the second adjustment.
interactions.priority: Adjust grind first. Temperature is your secondary lever.
interactions.exception: For dark roasts, lower temp first (to avoid burning) and only then adjust grind.

interactions.rule: In percolation methods (V60, Chemex, Kalita), grind size directly controls brew time. They are NOT independent — finer grind = longer drawdown.
interactions.implication: You cannot independently set both. If your V60 is draining in 2 minutes with a 3:30 target, grind finer. The time will increase as a consequence.

interactions.rule: In immersion methods (French press, clever dripper, AeroPress), grind and time ARE independent. You can set each separately.
interactions.implication: This makes immersion methods easier to dial in — change time without changing grind, or vice versa.

interactions.rule: Ratio changes STRENGTH. Grind changes EXTRACTION. These solve different problems. Weak coffee needs more coffee (ratio). Sour coffee needs finer grind (extraction). A common mistake is adding more coffee to fix sourness — this makes it stronger but still sour.
interactions.diagnostic: If coffee is weak AND sour: fix grind first (extraction), then adjust ratio (strength). If coffee is strong but sour: definitely a grind/temp problem, not ratio.

interactions.rule: Dark roasts are more soluble than light roasts. The same grind+temp that works for a light roast will over-extract a dark roast.
interactions.light_roast: Use hotter water (96-100C), can grind finer, longer brew time is fine
interactions.dark_roast: Use cooler water (85-92C), grind slightly coarser, shorter contact time
interactions.notes: This is the #1 reason people get bitter coffee with dark roasts — they use the same recipe that works for lighter coffees.

interactions.rule: More agitation + finer grind compounds extraction rapidly. If you stir aggressively with a fine grind, you will almost certainly over-extract.
interactions.safe_approach: If you increase agitation, consider going slightly coarser to compensate. Hoffmann's V60 technique uses a swirl (moderate agitation) with medium-fine grind as a balanced pairing.

interactions.rule: Bad water can make perfect technique taste wrong. Very soft water (RO) will taste sour/empty regardless of grind or temp. Very hard water will taste bitter/chalky regardless of how coarse you grind.
interactions.diagnostic: If you've adjusted grind, temp, and ratio and STILL can't get a balanced cup — it's probably your water. Switch to good quality filtered or bottled water and try again.

interactions.rule: In pour-over, total brew time is your feedback signal. It tells you if grind is right without needing to taste.

targets.french_press: 4:00 standard, up to 8-10 for Hoffmann method

interactions.notes: If V60 drains in under 2 minutes, grind finer. If it takes over 4 minutes, grind coarser. Taste confirms, time guides.

adjustment_priority.description: When something tastes wrong, adjust variables in this order. Fix one thing at a time, taste, then move to the next.

general_order.why: Highest impact, easiest to change between brews, affects extraction most directly.
Second lever for extraction. Especially important for dark roasts where grind alone may not solve bitterness.
Fixes strength (weak/strong) but not extraction (sour/bitter). Only adjust after extraction is balanced.
In immersion methods, this is a direct lever. In percolation, it's a consequence of grind — don't fight it.
Fine-tuning. Keep it consistent first, then experiment if other variables are dialed in.
Last resort — but if nothing else works, this is almost certainly the problem. Check before buying a new grinder.

espresso.notes: In espresso, grind is king. Ratio (dose in / liquid out) is second. Temp is usually fixed by the machine. Time is a RESULT of grind — target 25-35 seconds for most recipes.

french_press.notes: Immersion method — time is your primary lever since grind doesn't affect flow. Coarser grind = less sediment, but Hoffmann actually suggests medium grind with longer steep.

aeropress.notes: AeroPress is the most forgiving brewer. Grind and time are both independently adjustable. Hard to make truly bad coffee with it.

moka_pot.notes: Heat control is critical — low and steady. Grind should be medium-fine (NOT espresso fine). Remove from heat the moment coffee starts sputtering. Pre-heat water in the base to reduce time grounds spend on heat.

one_change_at_a_time.rule: Never adjust more than one variable between brews. If you change grind AND temp simultaneously, you cannot know which change caused the improvement (or made it worse).
one_change_at_a_time.exception: When switching to a completely different bean or roast level, reset multiple variables to known starting points for that roast profile, then dial in from there.

the_extraction_spectrum.description: What extracts at each stage, in order. Early extraction pulls acids, middle pulls sugars and aromatics, late extraction pulls bitter phenols and astringent compounds.

stages.compounds: fruit acids, citric acid, malic acid
stages.taste: sour, bright, thin, acidic, no sweetness

stages.compounds: sugars, caramels, chocolate, fruit sweetness, Maillard products
stages.taste: balanced, sweet, complex, pleasant acidity with body

stages.compounds: tannins, phenols, woody/ashy compounds, caffeine (more dissolves)
stages.taste: bitter, astringent, harsh, dry finish, hollow

the_extraction_spectrum.notes: The goal is not to hit exactly 20% — it's to get into the 18-22% range where sweetness and complexity live. The exact sweet spot depends on the bean, roast, and personal preference.
